title: The Cock-Fighterï؟½s Garland.[1]
content:
آ آ آ آ Museï؟½hide his name of whom I sing,
آ آ آ آ Lest his surviving house thou bring
آ آ آ آ For his sake into scorn,
آ آ آ آ Nor speak the school from which he drew
آ آ آ آ The much or little that he knew,
آ آ آ آ Nor place where he was born.


آ آ آ آ That such a man once was, may seem
آ آ آ آ Worthy of record (if the theme
آ آ آ آ Perchance may credit win)
آ آ آ آ For proof to man, what man may prove,
آ آ آ آ If grace depart, and demons move
آ آ آ آ The source of guilt within.


آ آ آ آ This man (for since the howling wild
آ آ آ آ Disclaims him, man he must be styled)
آ آ آ آ Wanted no good below,
آ آ آ آ Gentle he was, if gentle birth
آ آ آ آ Could make him such, and he had worth,
آ آ آ آ If wealth can worth bestow.


آ آ آ آ In social talk and ready jest,
آ آ آ آ He shone superior at the feast,
آ آ آ آ And qualities of mind,
آ آ آ آ Illustrious in the eyes of those
آ آ آ آ Whose gay society he chose,
آ آ آ آ Possessï؟½d of every kind.


آ آ آ آ Methinks I see him powderï؟½d red,
آ آ آ آ With bushy locks on his well-dressï؟½d head
آ آ آ آ Wingï؟½d broad on either side,
آ آ آ آ The mossy rosebud not so sweet;
آ آ آ آ His steeds superb, his carriage neat,
آ آ آ آ As luxury could provide.


آ آ آ آ Can such be cruel? Such can be
آ آ آ آ Cruel as hell, and so was he;
آ آ آ آ A tyrant entertainï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With barbarous sports, whose fell delight
آ آ آ آ Was to encourage mortal fight
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twixt birds to battle trainï؟½d.


آ آ آ آ One featherï؟½d champion he possessï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ His darling far beyond the rest,
آ آ آ آ Which never knew disgrace,
آ آ آ آ Nor eï؟½er had fought but he made flow
آ آ آ آ The life-blood of his fiercest foe,
آ آ آ آ The Cï؟½sar of his race.


آ آ آ آ It chanced at last, when, on a day,
آ آ آ آ He pushï؟½d him to the desperate fray,
آ آ آ آ His courage droopï؟½d, he fled.
آ آ آ آ The master stormï؟½d, the prize was lost,
آ آ آ آ And, instant, frantic at the cost,
آ آ آ آ He doomï؟½d his favourite dead.


آ آ آ آ He seized him fast, and from the pit
آ آ آ آ Flew to the kitchen, snatchï؟½d the spit,
آ آ آ آ And, Bring me cord, he cried;
آ آ آ آ The cord was brought, and, at his word,
آ آ آ آ To that dire implement the bird,
آ آ آ آ Alive and struggling, tied.


آ آ آ آ The horrid sequel asks a veil;
آ آ آ آ And all the terrors of the tale
آ آ آ آ That can be shall be sunkï؟½
آ آ آ آ Led by the suffererï؟½s screams aright
آ آ آ آ His shockï؟½d companions view the sight,
آ آ آ آ And him with fury drunk.


آ آ آ آ All, suppliant, beg a milder fate
آ آ آ آ For the old warrior at the grate:
آ آ آ آ He, deaf to pityï؟½s call,
آ آ آ آ Whirlï؟½d round him rapid as a wheel
آ آ آ آ His culinary club of steel,
آ آ آ آ Death menacing on all.


آ آ آ آ But vengeance hung not far remote,
آ آ آ آ For while he stretchï؟½d his clamorous throat,
آ آ آ آ And heaven and earth defied,
آ آ آ آ Big with a curse too closely pent,
آ آ آ آ That struggled vainly for a vent,
آ آ آ آ He totterï؟½d, reelï؟½d, and died.


آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis not for us, with rash surmise,
آ آ آ آ To point the judgment of the skies;
آ آ آ آ But judgments plain as this,
آ آ آ آ That, sent for manï؟½s instruction, bring
آ آ آ آ A written label on their wing,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis hard to read amiss.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Cowper, titled "The Cock-Fighterï؟½s Garland.[1]", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
